content.full_text:
Farn Polrrrcal PRACrrcBs CoMMrssroN
P.O. Box 807 o 428 J Street o Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 e Fax (916) 322-0886
May 3,2002
Lori Jacobs
Scott & Cronin, LLP
330 Encinitas Blvd., Suite 101
Encinitas, CA 92024-3723
Re
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 4-02-090
This is in response to your letter requesting advice on behalf of the San Diego
Board of Realtors (Board) and its sponsored political action committee (PAC) regarding
the commingling of contributions received by the PAC with the Board's general account
funds, under the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1
QUESTION
May the Board receive contributions raised specifically for the PAC into the
Board's general fund account, then transfer these contributions into the PAC account as
long as records are kept as to the contributors and the Board is reported as an
intermediary for the funds?
CONCLUSION
The Board may receive contributions earmarked for the PAC into the Board's
general fund account and transfer the contributions into the PAC account. As noted,
information on the contributors must be maintained and the Board must be identified as
the intermediary for the funds on the PAC's campaign reports.
' 
Government Code sections 8i000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear attttle 2, sections
18109 - 1899'7, of the California Code of Regulations.
ej


Lori Jacobs
A-02-090
Page Two
FACTS
The San Diego Board of Realtors has a sponsored PAC. From time to time, the
Board will raise funds from special events and sponsorships that are specifically
earmarked for its PAC. Additionally, the PAC receives contributions from membership
dues. In a telephone conversation on April 30,2002, you stated that, while the PAC has
been dormant for some time, it may contribute to state candidates in the future. In
addition, you stated that you do not anticipate the PAC receiving $5,000 or more from a
single source in a calendar year.
ANALYSIS
The Act does not regulate the commingling of an organization's general fund
money with its sponsored PAC's funds. The Act does provide that one person might act
as an intermediary by collecting a contribution for the benefit of another person. (Section
84302; Reg 18432 5.) We have advised that a person will be considered an intermediary
i[ at the time of the contribution, the original contributor knowingly and unambiguously
earmarks the contribution to be deposited with the second person. (See, e.g., Miller
Advice Letter, No. A-99- 254; Olson Advice Letter, No. I-97-327; Krausse Advice
Letter, No 4-96-349 )
A payment is earmarked for political purposes when, at the time of rnaking the
payment, the donor knows or has reason to know that the funds with which the payment
will be commingled, will be used to make contributions or expenditures. (Reg.
18215(b)(1).) Factors relevant for determining whether a donor has reason to know that
his or her payment will be used for political purposes can include the established practice
of the organization coliecting the payment, and any representations made by the
organization when the payment is made. (See, e.g., McCray Advice Letter, No. A-89-
334 )
In the case of a sponsored committee, such as the San Diego Board of Realtors'
PAC, please keep in mind that special reporting rules apply to contributions received
through intermediaries. Typically, a committee must itemize or report detailed
information about the intermediary of a contribution only if the cumulative amount of the
contribution from the original contributor equals $ 100 or more in a calendar year.
(Section 84302; Cathcart Advice Letter, No. A-00- 101 ) However, a sponsored
committee must report a sponsor acting as an intermediary of a lump sum payment of
$100 or more regardless of whether any one member contributed S100 or more. (Reg.
l8a1e(c)(3) )


Lori Jacobs
A-02-090
Page Three
Please review the enclosed regulations I 8419 and 18421. 1 for additional
information on reporting requirements for sponsored committees. Regulation 18421 . i (d),
in particular, will assist you in determining when a contribution has been received by the
PAC for reporting purposes.
In addition, if the PAC wants to qualify as a small contributor committee, it must
first meet the criteria found in section 85302.2
If you have any additional questions, please contact me at (566) 275-3772.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General
By
S. Moen, PhD
P olitical Reform Consultant
Enclosures
2 
Section 85203 defines a "small contributor committee" as a committee that "...has been rn
existence for at least six months; receives contributions from 100 or more persons, (whereby) [n]o one
person has contributed to flie committee more than two hundred dollars ($200) per calendar year; and, [the
committee] makes contributions to five or more candidates." Section 85302 states that a small contnlbutor
committee may not contribute more than $6,000 per election to any candidate for elective state office other
theut a cardidate for statewide elective office. Except for candidates for Governor, a small contributor
committee may not contribute more than $10,000 to any candidate for statewide elective office per election.
Such committees may not contribute more than $20,000 to candidates for Governor per election. If the
Board wants to register as a small contributor committee and meets the criteria listed in section 85203, it
rnust arnend its stateneft of organization (Form 410) prior to making contributions that exceed the limits in
section 85301
